sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Mr. Watson, an 86-year-old male, returns for follow-up after undergoing shunt surgery on August 1, 2020, for adult hydrocephalus.
He initially presented with 8-10 years of progressive gait impairment, cognitive impairment, and decreased bladder control.

Since surgery, he has experienced improvement in his symptoms, with his walking now improved to the point where he can walk to the dining room without assistance, although he still requires occasional help.
His bladder control has also improved, but he now wears diapers.
He reports no issues with sores or painful urination.
He also reports improved cognitive function, stating he no longer feels lost when conversing with others.
His daughter, Theresa, corroborates this improvement.
Past medical history: Adult hydrocephalus with spinal catheter protocol since June 2020, shunt surgery on August 1, 2020.
Review of systems: Negative for headaches, pain, fever, unintentional weight loss, recent falls, hallucinations, depression, excessive worrying, or mood swings.
Positive for impaired ability to carry out daily functions, urinary incontinence, and wearing a diaper.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/80, heart rate 64, respirations 18, weight 174 pounds, and pain 0 out of 10.
Physical exam: The shunt site was clean, dry, and intact with a confirmed setting of 2.0.
Mr. Watson is a pleasant and cooperative man who is able to converse easily, although his daughter adds some details.
Cranial nerves, pupils are equal, eoms are intact, face symmetric, no dysarthria, motor normal for bulk and strength, coordination slow from finger-to-nose.
Test Results: The MMSE score was 26 out of 30 on the spelling test and 25 out of 30 on the calculation test, with slight improvement from his initial score.

Gait tested using the Tinetti assessment tool resulted in a score of 6-8 out of 12 for gait and 12 out of 16 for balance, for a total score of 18-20 out of 28, which is slightly improved from his initial score.

The CT scan from 10/15/2020 shows a frontal hornstan at the level of firm and of menroma of 4.6 cm with a third ventricular contour that is flat with a span of 10 mm, with a tiny amount of blood in the right frontal region and a tiny subdermal collection.
Assessment / Problem List:

Assessment: Mr. Watson is a 86-year-old male with adult hydrocephalus who has experienced significant improvement in his symptoms since shunt surgery, with improved gait and cognitive function, although he still requires occasional assistance and wears diapers for bladder control.
Problem list: Adult hydrocephalus; Gait impairment; Urinary incontinence; Cognitive impairment.
Plan:
I plan to continue monitoring Mr. Watson's condition and schedule a follow-up appointment in 3 months to reassess his progress.
I will also recommend that he enroll in a driver's safety program to assess his ability to drive safely.
Follow-up: Schedule a follow-up appointment in 3 months to reassess Mr. Watson's progress and discuss the possibility of him driving again.
